questions.title: Two Sum
questions.problem: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers whose sum equals target. Each input has exactly one valid pair, and the same element cannot be used twice.
questions.explanation: Two Sum is a easy Arrays interview problem. Focus on the input contract, edge cases, and the optimal pattern before writing code.
examples: Input: nums = [2,7,11,15], target = 9 -> Output: [0,1]
Input: nums = [3,2,4], target = 6 -> Output: [1,2]
constraints: 2 <= nums.length <= 100000
Return any valid pair if multiple pairs exist
hints: Store each number's index while scanning.
For every value x, check whether target - x has already appeared.
questions.solution: Use a hash map from value to index. Scan the array once; before inserting nums[i], check whether target - nums[i] exists in the map. This returns the pair in linear time.
questions.referenceNote: Content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.

questions.title: Valid Parentheses
questions.problem: Given a string containing only brackets '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine whether every opening bracket is closed by the same type of bracket in the correct order.
questions.explanation: Valid Parentheses is a easy Stacks interview problem. Focus on the input contract, edge cases, and the optimal pattern before writing code.
examples: Input: s = "()[]{}" -> Output: true
Input: s = "(]" -> Output: false
constraints: s contains only bracket characters
hints: The most recent open bracket must be closed first.
Use a stack and a closing-to-opening map.
questions.solution: Push opening brackets onto a stack. For every closing bracket, the top of the stack must match its corresponding opener. At the end, the stack must be empty.
questions.referenceNote: Content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.

questions.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
questions.problem: Given an array prices where prices[i] is the stock price on day i, choose one day to buy and a later day to sell so profit is maximized. Return the maximum profit, or 0 if no profit is possible.
questions.explanation: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock is a easy Arrays interview problem. Focus on the input contract, edge cases, and the optimal pattern before writing code.
examples: Input: prices = [7,1,5,3,6,4] -> Output: 5
Input: prices = [7,6,4,3,1] -> Output: 0
constraints: 1 <= prices.length <= 100000
hints: Track the cheapest price seen so far.
At each day, calculate profit if sold today.
questions.solution: Scan once. Keep minPrice and maxProfit. For every price, update maxProfit with price - minPrice, then update minPrice.
questions.referenceNote: Content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.

questions.title: Number of Islands
questions.problem: Given a 2D grid of '1' land and '0' water, count how many islands exist. An island is connected horizontally or vertically.
questions.explanation: Number of Islands is a medium Graphs interview problem. Focus on the input contract, edge cases, and the optimal pattern before writing code.
examples: Input: grid = [[1,1],[1,1]] -> Output: 1
constraints: 1 <= rows, cols <= 300
grid contains only 0/1 or '0'/'1' values
hints: When land is found, flood-fill the full connected component.
DFS, BFS, or Union Find work.
questions.solution: Scan every cell. When unvisited land is found, increment count and run DFS/BFS to mark all connected land visited.
questions.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) worst case for recursion/queue

questions.title: LRU Cache
questions.problem: Design a data structure that supports get(key) and put(key, value) in O(1) average time while evicting the least recently used key when capacity is exceeded.
questions.explanation: LRU Cache is a medium Design interview problem. Focus on the input contract, edge cases, and the optimal pattern before writing code.
examples: Input: put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(3,3), get(2) -> Output: 1, -1
Capacity 2 should evict the key that has not been used for the longest time.
constraints: 1 <= capacity <= 100000
Operations should be O(1) average time
hints: Hash map gives O(1) lookup.
Doubly linked list gives O(1) move-to-front and eviction.
questions.solution: Use a hash map from key to node and a doubly linked list ordered by recency. Move accessed nodes to the front and remove from the tail on eviction.

questions.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
questions.referenceNote: Content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.
questions.problem: Given the heads of two sorted linked lists, merge them into one sorted linked list by reusing the existing nodes, and return the head of the merged list.
questions.explanation: Merge Two Sorted Lists is a easy Linked Lists interview problem. Focus on the input contract, edge cases, and the optimal pattern before writing code.
examples: Input: list1 = [], list2 = [0] -> Output: [0]
constraints: 0 <= list length <= 50000
Both input lists are sorted in non-decreasing order
hints: Use a dummy head to simplify pointer handling.
Always attach the smaller current node.
questions.solution: Maintain a tail pointer on a dummy list. Compare the current nodes of both lists, attach the smaller node, and advance that list. Attach the remaining list at the end.

questions.title: Maximum Subarray
questions.referenceNote: Content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.
questions.problem: Given an integer array nums, find the contiguous non-empty subarray with the largest possible sum and return that sum.
questions.explanation: Maximum Subarray is a easy Arrays interview problem. Focus on the input contract, edge cases, and the optimal pattern before writing code.
examples: Input: nums = [5,4,-1,7,8] -> Output: 23
constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 100000
hints: A negative running sum hurts the next subarray.
Keep the best sum ending at the current index.
questions.solution: Use Kadane's algorithm. current = max(nums[i], current + nums[i]); best = max(best, current).

questions.title: Binary Search
questions.referenceNote: Content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.
questions.problem: Given a sorted integer array nums and a target, return the index of target if it exists. Otherwise return -1.
questions.explanation: Binary Search is a easy Binary Search interview problem. Focus on the input contract, edge cases, and the optimal pattern before writing code.
examples: Input: nums = [-1,0,3,5,9,12], target = 9 -> Output: 4
Input: nums = [-1,0,3,5,9,12], target = 2 -> Output: -1
constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 100000
nums is sorted in ascending order
All values fit in 32-bit signed integer range
hints: Keep low and high pointers.
Use mid = low + Math.floor((high - low) / 2) to avoid overflow in some languages.
questions.solution: Repeatedly compare target with nums[mid]. If equal, return mid. If target is smaller, search left half; otherwise search right half.

questions.solution: Iterate through the list. Save next, point current.next to previous, then move previous and current forward.

contentItems.question: Two Sum
contentItems.sourceLine: [Easy] Two Sum (Asked in: Amazon, TCS, Accenture)
contentItems.referenceNote: This content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.
contentItems.title: Two Sum
contentItems.problem: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers whose sum equals target. Each input has exactly one valid pair, and the same element cannot be used twice.
examples: Input: nums = [2,7,11,15], target = 9 -> Output: [0,1]
Input: nums = [3,2,4], target = 6 -> Output: [1,2]
constraints: 2 <= nums.length <= 100000
Return any valid pair if multiple pairs exist
hints: Store each number's index while scanning.
For every value x, check whether target - x has already appeared.
contentItems.solution: Use a hash map from value to index. Scan the array once; before inserting nums[i], check whether target - nums[i] exists in the map. This returns the pair in linear time.
contentItems.preview: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers whose sum equals target. Each input has exactly one valid pair, and the same element cannot be used twice.

contentItems.question: Valid Parentheses
contentItems.sourceLine: [Easy] Valid Parentheses (Asked in: Facebook, Infosys)
contentItems.referenceNote: This content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.
contentItems.title: Valid Parentheses
contentItems.problem: Given a string containing only brackets '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine whether every opening bracket is closed by the same type of bracket in the correct order.
examples: Input: s = "()[]{}" -> Output: true
Input: s = "(]" -> Output: false
constraints: s contains only bracket characters
hints: The most recent open bracket must be closed first.
Use a stack and a closing-to-opening map.
contentItems.solution: Push opening brackets onto a stack. For every closing bracket, the top of the stack must match its corresponding opener. At the end, the stack must be empty.
contentItems.preview: Given a string containing only brackets '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine whether every opening bracket is closed by the same type of bracket in the correct order.

contentItems.question: Merge Two Sorted Lists
contentItems.sourceLine: [Easy] Merge Two Sorted Lists (Asked in: Microsoft, Amazon)
contentItems.referenceNote: This content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.
contentItems.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
contentItems.problem: Given the heads of two sorted linked lists, merge them into one sorted linked list by reusing the existing nodes, and return the head of the merged list.
examples: Input: list1 = [], list2 = [0] -> Output: [0]
constraints: 0 <= list length <= 50000
Both input lists are sorted in non-decreasing order
hints: Use a dummy head to simplify pointer handling.
Always attach the smaller current node.
contentItems.solution: Maintain a tail pointer on a dummy list. Compare the current nodes of both lists, attach the smaller node, and advance that list. Attach the remaining list at the end.
contentItems.preview: Given the heads of two sorted linked lists, merge them into one sorted linked list by reusing the existing nodes, and return the head of the merged list.

contentItems.question: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
contentItems.sourceLine: [Easy] Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock (Asked in: Amazon, Nvidia)
contentItems.referenceNote: This content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.
contentItems.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
contentItems.problem: Given an array prices where prices[i] is the stock price on day i, choose one day to buy and a later day to sell so profit is maximized. Return the maximum profit, or 0 if no profit is possible.
examples: Input: prices = [7,1,5,3,6,4] -> Output: 5
Input: prices = [7,6,4,3,1] -> Output: 0
constraints: 1 <= prices.length <= 100000
hints: Track the cheapest price seen so far.
At each day, calculate profit if sold today.
contentItems.solution: Scan once. Keep minPrice and maxProfit. For every price, update maxProfit with price - minPrice, then update minPrice.
contentItems.preview: Given an array prices where prices[i] is the stock price on day i, choose one day to buy and a later day to sell so profit is maximized. Return the maximum profit, or 0 if no profit is possible.

contentItems.question: Valid Palindrome
contentItems.sourceLine: [Easy] Valid Palindrome (Asked in: Facebook, TCS)
contentItems.referenceNote: This content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.
contentItems.title: Valid Palindrome
contentItems.problem: Given input data for "Valid Palindrome", write an efficient function that returns the exact result requested by the interview problem. Handle normal, boundary, and duplicate cases.
examples: Input: representative small case -> Output: expected result
Input: edge case with duplicates/empty/minimum values -> Output: correct boundary result
constraints: 1 <= n <= 100000 unless stated otherwise
Aim for O(n) or O(n log n) before accepting brute force
Handle duplicates and negative values when relevant
hints: Identify whether the core pattern is hashing, two pointers, sorting, stack, or sliding window.
Write down the invariant maintained by your loop.
contentItems.solution: Start from the brute force idea, then optimize using the strongest matching pattern: hash map for lookups, two pointers for ordered data, stack for nearest/open-close relations, or sliding window for contiguous ranges.
contentItems.preview: Given input data for "Valid Palindrome", write an efficient function that returns the exact result requested by the interview problem. Handle normal, boundary, and duplicate cases.

contentItems.question: Invert Binary Tree
contentItems.sourceLine: [Easy] Invert Binary Tree (Asked in: Google, Accenture)
contentItems.referenceNote: This content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.
contentItems.title: Invert Binary Tree
contentItems.problem: Given a Trees structure for "Invert Binary Tree", return the required traversal, validation, query result, or transformed structure while preserving the problem invariants.
examples: Input: root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7] -> Output: result based on traversal/validation task
Input: root = [] -> Output: empty/default result
constraints: Node values fit in integer range
Avoid stack overflow for very deep structures where needed
hints: Recursive DFS is natural for most tree tasks.
Use BFS when the question asks for level-wise behavior.
contentItems.solution: Choose DFS, BFS, heap operations, or trie traversal based on the structure. Track the exact invariant the question asks you to prove or compute.
contentItems.preview: Given a Trees structure for "Invert Binary Tree", return the required traversal, validation, query result, or transformed structure while preserving the problem invariants.

contentItems.question: Maximum Subarray
contentItems.sourceLine: [Easy] Maximum Subarray (Asked in: LinkedIn, Infosys)
contentItems.referenceNote: This content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.
contentItems.title: Maximum Subarray
contentItems.problem: Given an integer array nums, find the contiguous non-empty subarray with the largest possible sum and return that sum.
examples: Input: nums = [5,4,-1,7,8] -> Output: 23
constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 100000
hints: A negative running sum hurts the next subarray.
Keep the best sum ending at the current index.
contentItems.solution: Use Kadane's algorithm. current = max(nums[i], current + nums[i]); best = max(best, current).
contentItems.preview: Given an integer array nums, find the contiguous non-empty subarray with the largest possible sum and return that sum.

contentItems.question: Binary Search
contentItems.sourceLine: [Easy] Binary Search (Asked in: Apple, TCS)
contentItems.referenceNote: This content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.
contentItems.title: Binary Search
contentItems.problem: Given a sorted integer array nums and a target, return the index of target if it exists. Otherwise return -1.
examples: Input: nums = [-1,0,3,5,9,12], target = 9 -> Output: 4
Input: nums = [-1,0,3,5,9,12], target = 2 -> Output: -1
constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 100000
nums is sorted in ascending order
All values fit in 32-bit signed integer range
hints: Keep low and high pointers.
Use mid = low + Math.floor((high - low) / 2) to avoid overflow in some languages.
contentItems.solution: Repeatedly compare target with nums[mid]. If equal, return mid. If target is smaller, search left half; otherwise search right half.
contentItems.preview: Given a sorted integer array nums and a target, return the index of target if it exists. Otherwise return -1.

contentItems.question: Reverse Linked List
contentItems.sourceLine: [Easy] Reverse Linked List (Asked in: Amazon, Nvidia)
contentItems.referenceNote: This content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.
contentItems.title: Reverse Linked List
contentItems.problem: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list and return the new head.
examples: Input: head = [] -> Output: []
constraints: 0 <= number of nodes <= 100000
hints: Keep previous, current, and next pointers.
Reverse one link at a time.
contentItems.solution: Iterate through the list. Save next, point current.next to previous, then move previous and current forward.
contentItems.preview: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list and return the new head.

contentItems.question: LRU Cache
contentItems.sourceLine: [Medium] LRU Cache (Asked in: Amazon, Nvidia, TCS)
contentItems.referenceNote: This content is extracted from the uploaded Ultimate Tech Interview Master Guide. Company tags are preparation signals and can vary by year, role, campus, team, and interviewer.
contentItems.title: LRU Cache
contentItems.problem: Design a data structure that supports get(key) and put(key, value) in O(1) average time while evicting the least recently used key when capacity is exceeded.
examples: Input: put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(3,3), get(2) -> Output: 1, -1
Capacity 2 should evict the key that has not been used for the longest time.
constraints: 1 <= capacity <= 100000
Operations should be O(1) average time
hints: Hash map gives O(1) lookup.
Doubly linked list gives O(1) move-to-front and eviction.
contentItems.solution: Use a hash map from key to node and a doubly linked list ordered by recency. Move accessed nodes to the front and remove from the tail on eviction.
contentItems.preview: Design a data structure that supports get(key) and put(key, value) in O(1) average time while evicting the least recently used key when capacity is exceeded.

contentItems.title: Number of Islands
contentItems.problem: Given a 2D grid of '1' land and '0' water, count how many islands exist. An island is connected horizontally or vertically.
examples: Input: grid = [[1,1],[1,1]] -> Output: 1
constraints: 1 <= rows, cols <= 300
grid contains only 0/1 or '0'/'1' values
hints: When land is found, flood-fill the full connected component.
DFS, BFS, or Union Find work.

contentItems.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) worst case for recursion/queue

contentItems.title: Prefix Sum Range Query
contentItems.question: You need to answer many range-sum queries on a static integer array. Which preprocessing technique gives O(1) query time after O(n) preprocessing?
correctAnswer: Prefix sum array
contentItems.explanation: A prefix sum array stores cumulative sums, so range sum l..r is prefix[r] - prefix[l - 1]. Sorting destroys original positions, a BST helps ordered lookup not range sums by index, and scanning each query is O(n).
contentItems.sourceLine: You need to answer many range-sum queries on a static integer array. Which preprocessing technique gives O(1) query time after O(n) preprocessing?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Next Greater Element Structure
contentItems.question: Which data structure is commonly used to solve Next Greater Element in O(n) time?
correctAnswer: Monotonic stack
contentItems.explanation: A monotonic stack keeps unresolved elements in decreasing order and resolves them when a greater element appears. A queue does not maintain the needed ordering, a trie is for prefixes, and DSU is for connectivity.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which data structure is commonly used to solve Next Greater Element in O(n) time?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Two Sum Lookup
contentItems.question: In the standard Two Sum problem, why is a hash map used?
options: To store complements and find matches in O(1) average time
To convert the problem into recursion
correctAnswer: To store complements and find matches in O(1) average time
contentItems.explanation: A hash map stores previously seen values or complements for constant-average lookup. It does not sort, it uses O(n) memory, and recursion is unrelated to the optimal lookup pattern.
contentItems.sourceLine: In the standard Two Sum problem, why is a hash map used?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: BFS Shortest Path
contentItems.question: Why does BFS find the shortest path in an unweighted graph?
options: It always chooses the smallest edge weight
It explores nodes level by level from the source
correctAnswer: It explores nodes level by level from the source
contentItems.explanation: In an unweighted graph, each BFS level represents one more edge from the source, so first visit gives shortest distance. It does not use edge weights, recursion depth is DFS-like, and sorted adjacency is not required.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why does BFS find the shortest path in an unweighted graph?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Dynamic Programming Signal
contentItems.question: Which pair of properties usually indicates that dynamic programming may be useful?
options: Only constant memory and no choices
correctAnswer: Overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure
contentItems.explanation: DP fits when subproblems repeat and optimal answers compose from smaller optimal answers. Sorted input hints binary search/two pointers, XOR hints bit tricks, and constant memory is an optimization not the reason for DP.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which pair of properties usually indicates that dynamic programming may be useful?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Union-Find Use Case
contentItems.question: Which problem is best suited for Disjoint Set Union?
options: Detecting connected components while merging edges
correctAnswer: Detecting connected components while merging edges
contentItems.explanation: DSU efficiently supports union and find operations for dynamic connectivity. Palindromes need string/DP methods, expressions need stacks/parsing, and LRU needs a hash map plus linked list.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which problem is best suited for Disjoint Set Union?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: LRU Cache Design
contentItems.question: What data structures are typically combined to implement an O(1) LRU cache?
options: Hash map and doubly linked list
correctAnswer: Hash map and doubly linked list
contentItems.explanation: The hash map gives O(1) key lookup and the doubly linked list supports O(1) move-to-front and eviction from the tail. The other combinations do not support both lookup and recency updates in O(1).
contentItems.sourceLine: What data structures are typically combined to implement an O(1) LRU cache?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Dijkstra Limitation
contentItems.question: Why is Dijkstra's algorithm unsafe on graphs with negative edge weights?
options: A node marked final may later receive a shorter path through a negative edge
It requires all edges to have weight one
correctAnswer: A node marked final may later receive a shorter path through a negative edge
contentItems.explanation: Dijkstra assumes once the smallest tentative distance is extracted, it is final. Negative edges can violate that assumption. Priority queues are allowed, it works on general graphs, and unit weights are specifically the BFS case.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why is Dijkstra's algorithm unsafe on graphs with negative edge weights?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Process vs Thread
contentItems.question: What is the key difference between a process and a thread?
options: Threads have separate address spaces by default
Processes are independent execution units; threads share the process address space
Threads always run on different CPUs
correctAnswer: Processes are independent execution units; threads share the process address space
contentItems.explanation: A process has its own address space and resources, while threads within a process share memory. Threads do not have separate address spaces by default, both can run concurrently, and CPU placement is scheduler-dependent.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is the key difference between a process and a thread?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Context Switch Cost
contentItems.question: Why is a context switch considered overhead?
options: The CPU saves and restores execution state instead of doing useful process work
correctAnswer: The CPU saves and restores execution state instead of doing useful process work
contentItems.explanation: Context switching saves registers/state and loads another task's state, which consumes CPU time. It is not application logic, does not delete memory, and does not disable interrupts forever.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why is a context switch considered overhead?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Deadlock Conditions
contentItems.question: Which set correctly names the Coffman deadlock conditions?
options: Mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait
correctAnswer: Mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait
contentItems.explanation: All four Coffman conditions must hold for deadlock. The other choices are memory management, process system calls, or file permissions rather than deadlock conditions.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which set correctly names the Coffman deadlock conditions?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Semaphore vs Mutex
contentItems.question: When is a counting semaphore more appropriate than a mutex?
options: When multiple identical resource units must be controlled
When only one owner may unlock a critical section
correctAnswer: When multiple identical resource units must be controlled
contentItems.explanation: A counting semaphore tracks available resource permits. A mutex is for exclusive ownership of one critical section, no synchronization needs neither, and virtual memory is unrelated.
contentItems.sourceLine: When is a counting semaphore more appropriate than a mutex?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Page Fault Meaning
contentItems.question: What happens during a page fault?
options: The CPU detects a syntax error
The referenced virtual page is not currently in physical memory and the OS must handle it
correctAnswer: The referenced virtual page is not currently in physical memory and the OS must handle it
contentItems.explanation: A page fault traps into the OS so the needed page can be loaded or access can be rejected. It is not a syntax error or DNS issue, and it does not always terminate the process.
contentItems.sourceLine: What happens during a page fault?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Thrashing
contentItems.question: What is thrashing in an operating system?
options: Excessive time spent paging instead of executing useful work
A faster CPU cache hit rate
correctAnswer: Excessive time spent paging instead of executing useful work
contentItems.explanation: Thrashing occurs when memory pressure causes constant page swaps, so progress collapses. It is not cache improvement, scheduling policy, or networking control.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is thrashing in an operating system?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Priority Inversion
contentItems.question: What problem does priority inheritance solve?
options: Low-priority task holding a resource needed by a high-priority task
correctAnswer: Low-priority task holding a resource needed by a high-priority task
contentItems.explanation: Priority inheritance temporarily boosts the resource holder so it can release the lock. The other options are storage, security, and networking issues.
contentItems.sourceLine: What problem does priority inheritance solve?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Copy-on-Write Fork
contentItems.question: Why do many Unix-like systems use copy-on-write after fork?
options: To copy every memory page immediately
To defer physical copying until a shared page is modified
To prevent child processes from running
correctAnswer: To defer physical copying until a shared page is modified
contentItems.explanation: Copy-on-write lets parent and child share pages until one writes, reducing fork cost. It avoids immediate copying, does not stop child execution, and is not encryption.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why do many Unix-like systems use copy-on-write after fork?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Primary Key Purpose
contentItems.question: What is the purpose of a primary key?
options: Uniquely identify each row in a table
correctAnswer: Uniquely identify each row in a table
contentItems.explanation: A primary key uniquely identifies table rows and cannot be null. It is not encryption, does not allow duplicates, and is not limited to text.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is the purpose of a primary key?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Index Trade-off
contentItems.question: What is a common trade-off of adding indexes?
options: Faster reads but extra storage and slower writes
Slower reads and faster writes always
correctAnswer: Faster reads but extra storage and slower writes
contentItems.explanation: Indexes speed lookups but must be maintained during writes and consume storage. They do not normalize schema or automatically remove duplicates.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is a common trade-off of adding indexes?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: WHERE vs HAVING
contentItems.question: When should HAVING be used instead of WHERE?
options: To filter rows before grouping only
correctAnswer: To filter aggregated groups
contentItems.explanation: WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after aggregation. Index creation and key definition are DDL operations.
contentItems.sourceLine: When should HAVING be used instead of WHERE?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Normalization Goal
contentItems.question: What is the main goal of database normalization?
correctAnswer: Reduce redundancy and update anomalies
contentItems.explanation: Normalization structures data to reduce duplication and inconsistencies. It does not intentionally duplicate data, disable relationships, or aim to slow queries.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is the main goal of database normalization?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Isolation Level Issue
contentItems.question: Which anomaly occurs when a transaction reads data written by another uncommitted transaction?
correctAnswer: Dirty read
contentItems.explanation: A dirty read observes uncommitted changes that may roll back. Phantom reads involve new rows matching a predicate, hash collisions are data structure issues, and dead packet is not a DB anomaly.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which anomaly occurs when a transaction reads data written by another uncommitted transaction?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: ACID Durability
contentItems.question: What does durability guarantee after a transaction commits?
options: Committed changes survive crashes according to the database guarantees
No user can read the data
correctAnswer: Committed changes survive crashes according to the database guarantees
contentItems.explanation: Durability means committed data persists despite failures. It says nothing about speed, automatic indexes, or access control.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does durability guarantee after a transaction commits?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: B-tree Index Strength
contentItems.question: Why are B-tree indexes commonly used in databases?
options: They support ordered lookups and range scans efficiently
correctAnswer: They support ordered lookups and range scans efficiently
contentItems.explanation: B-trees maintain sorted keys with balanced height, helping equality and range queries. They are not JSON-only, do not prevent all deadlocks, and do not replace logs.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why are B-tree indexes commonly used in databases?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Denormalization Reason
contentItems.question: Why might a team intentionally denormalize a database schema?
options: To improve read performance by reducing joins
correctAnswer: To improve read performance by reducing joins
contentItems.explanation: Denormalization may duplicate data to reduce join cost in read-heavy systems. It relaxes normalization, adds redundancy, and is separate from caching.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why might a team intentionally denormalize a database schema?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Current Directory Command
contentItems.question: Which Linux command prints the current working directory?
correctAnswer: pwd
contentItems.explanation: pwd prints the absolute path of the current directory. ls lists contents, cd changes directory, and mkdir creates directories.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which Linux command prints the current working directory?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Text Search Command
contentItems.question: Which command is best for searching lines that match a pattern in files?
correctAnswer: grep
contentItems.explanation: grep searches text using patterns. chmod changes permissions, touch creates/updates files, and kill sends signals.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which command is best for searching lines that match a pattern in files?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: chmod 755
contentItems.question: What does chmod 755 file typically mean?
options: Owner can read/write/execute; group and others can read/execute
correctAnswer: Owner can read/write/execute; group and others can read/execute
contentItems.explanation: 755 means rwx for owner and r-x for group/others. It does not grant everyone write, remove owner permissions, or imply root-only access.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does chmod 755 file typically mean?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Pipe Operator
contentItems.question: What does the pipe operator do in a shell command?
options: Passes stdout of one command as stdin to another
Runs only if previous command fails
correctAnswer: Passes stdout of one command as stdin to another
contentItems.explanation: A pipe connects command output to another command's input. It does not delete commands, failure logic is handled by ||, and chown changes ownership.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does the pipe operator do in a shell command?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Cron Use
contentItems.question: What is cron used for?
correctAnswer: Scheduling recurring commands
contentItems.explanation: cron runs commands on a time schedule. It is not only for kernel compilation, password encryption, or port listing.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is cron used for?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: systemd Service
contentItems.question: What is a systemd unit file used for?
options: Defining how services, sockets, timers, or other units are managed
correctAnswer: Defining how services, sockets, timers, or other units are managed
contentItems.explanation: systemd unit files describe managed resources and dependencies. They do not store cookies, define SQL schema, or compress images.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is a systemd unit file used for?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: sed vs awk
contentItems.question: Which statement best separates sed and awk?
options: sed is stream editing; awk is stronger for field-based processing and reports
correctAnswer: sed is stream editing; awk is stronger for field-based processing and reports
contentItems.explanation: sed edits streams line-by-line; awk provides fields, patterns, and actions for structured text. Neither is chmod, process manager, or package manager.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which statement best separates sed and awk?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Load Average
contentItems.question: What does Linux load average roughly represent?
options: Average number of runnable or uninterruptible tasks over time
correctAnswer: Average number of runnable or uninterruptible tasks over time
contentItems.explanation: Load average tracks runnable and uninterruptible tasks over 1/5/15 minutes. It is not CPU temperature, disk capacity, or package count.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does Linux load average roughly represent?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Load Balancer Purpose
contentItems.question: What is a load balancer used for?
options: Distributing incoming traffic across multiple backend instances
correctAnswer: Distributing incoming traffic across multiple backend instances
contentItems.explanation: Load balancers spread traffic and improve availability. Compilation, row encryption, and caching are separate concerns.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is a load balancer used for?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Cache Use
contentItems.question: Why add a cache in front of a slow data source?
options: To reduce latency and repeated load for frequently accessed data
To permanently replace the database in all cases
correctAnswer: To reduce latency and repeated load for frequently accessed data
contentItems.explanation: Caches speed repeated reads and reduce backend pressure. They do not always replace databases, block writes, or remove auth.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why add a cache in front of a slow data source?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Rate Limiter Design
contentItems.question: What is a common goal of rate limiting in system design?
options: Protect services from abuse or overload
correctAnswer: Protect services from abuse or overload
contentItems.explanation: Rate limiting controls request volume to preserve reliability and fairness. The other choices are harmful or unrelated.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is a common goal of rate limiting in system design?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Consistent Hashing
contentItems.question: Why is consistent hashing useful in distributed caches?
options: It reduces key movement when nodes join or leave
It removes the need for hashing
correctAnswer: It reduces key movement when nodes join or leave
contentItems.explanation: Consistent hashing maps keys to a ring so membership changes move fewer keys. It still hashes, does not prevent failures, and does not imply encryption.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why is consistent hashing useful in distributed caches?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Message Queue Benefit
contentItems.question: Why use a message queue between services?
options: To decouple producers and consumers and smooth traffic spikes
correctAnswer: To decouple producers and consumers and smooth traffic spikes
contentItems.explanation: Queues buffer work and decouple services. They do not force blocking, should not silently delete failures, and do not replace every database.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why use a message queue between services?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Idempotency
contentItems.question: Why are idempotency keys useful in payment APIs?
options: They prevent duplicate processing when clients retry requests
They remove the need for TLS
correctAnswer: They prevent duplicate processing when clients retry requests
contentItems.explanation: Idempotency keys let the server recognize retries and return the same result. They do not anonymize, replace TLS, or compress data.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why are idempotency keys useful in payment APIs?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Database Sharding
contentItems.question: What is database sharding?
options: Splitting data across multiple database nodes by a shard key
correctAnswer: Splitting data across multiple database nodes by a shard key
contentItems.explanation: Sharding partitions data horizontally. Read replicas copy data, encryption secures data, and indexes are separate.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is database sharding?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Backpressure
contentItems.question: What does backpressure do in a distributed system?
options: Signals upstream producers to slow down when downstream cannot keep up
Forces downstream to accept infinite load
correctAnswer: Signals upstream producers to slow down when downstream cannot keep up
contentItems.explanation: Backpressure prevents overload by slowing producers. It does not force infinite load, delete monitoring, or forbid retries permanently.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does backpressure do in a distributed system?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Shared Responsibility
contentItems.question: What does the cloud shared responsibility model mean?
options: Provider and customer split security responsibilities depending on service type
The provider handles every customer security task
The customer owns physical datacenter security
No one is responsible for backups
correctAnswer: Provider and customer split security responsibilities depending on service type
contentItems.explanation: Cloud providers secure infrastructure, while customers secure their data/configuration depending on IaaS/PaaS/SaaS. The provider does not handle every task, customers do not secure provider datacenters, and backups still need ownership.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does the cloud shared responsibility model mean?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Object Storage
contentItems.question: Which workload is object storage like Amazon S3 best suited for?
options: Storing images, backups, logs, and static assets as objects
correctAnswer: Storing images, backups, logs, and static assets as objects
contentItems.explanation: Object storage stores blobs with metadata at scale. It does not execute CPU instructions, replace RAM, or schedule OS processes.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which workload is object storage like Amazon S3 best suited for?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Auto Scaling Trigger
contentItems.question: Why is auto scaling used in cloud systems?
options: To adjust capacity based on demand
To remove the need for load balancing always
correctAnswer: To adjust capacity based on demand
contentItems.explanation: Auto scaling adds or removes capacity as load changes. It requires monitoring, often benefits from stateless design, and commonly works with load balancers.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why is auto scaling used in cloud systems?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: IaaS vs PaaS
contentItems.question: Which statement correctly describes PaaS compared with IaaS?
options: PaaS abstracts more infrastructure management from developers
PaaS always gives root access to physical hosts
correctAnswer: PaaS abstracts more infrastructure management from developers
contentItems.explanation: PaaS manages more runtime/platform concerns so developers focus on apps. It usually does not expose physical root, IaaS still requires more server management, and SaaS/IaaS differ significantly.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which statement correctly describes PaaS compared with IaaS?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Regions and Availability Zones
contentItems.question: Why deploy across multiple availability zones?
options: To improve resilience against zone-level failure
To force all traffic through one server
correctAnswer: To improve resilience against zone-level failure
contentItems.explanation: Multiple AZs reduce impact from a single zone outage. They do not remove auth, backups, or require single-server traffic.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why deploy across multiple availability zones?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Eventual Consistency
contentItems.question: What does eventual consistency mean?
options: Replicas may temporarily differ but converge if no new updates occur
Data is always immediately identical everywhere
Only one server can read data
correctAnswer: Replicas may temporarily differ but converge if no new updates occur
contentItems.explanation: Eventual consistency allows temporary divergence for availability/performance. Strong immediate equality is strong consistency, and reads/writes are still possible.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does eventual consistency mean?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Zero-Downtime Deployment
contentItems.question: Which practice best supports zero-downtime deployment?
options: Rolling or blue-green deployment with health checks
Manual database edits in production only
correctAnswer: Rolling or blue-green deployment with health checks
contentItems.explanation: Rolling/blue-green releases keep serving traffic while validating healthy instances. Stopping all servers causes downtime, manual DB edits are risky, and monitoring should stay enabled.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which practice best supports zero-downtime deployment?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Cloud Cost Control
contentItems.question: Which action most directly helps control unexpected cloud cost?
options: Budgets, alerts, right-sizing, and shutting down unused resources
correctAnswer: Budgets, alerts, right-sizing, and shutting down unused resources
contentItems.explanation: Cost control needs visibility and resource hygiene. Broad admin access increases risk, ignoring logs hides waste, and one region alone does not solve cost issues.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which action most directly helps control unexpected cloud cost?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: DNS Purpose
contentItems.question: What does DNS primarily do?
options: Maps domain names to IP addresses
correctAnswer: Maps domain names to IP addresses
contentItems.explanation: DNS resolves human-readable names to network addresses. Encryption, scheduling, and compilation are different systems.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does DNS primarily do?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: TCP vs UDP
contentItems.question: Which statement best describes TCP compared with UDP?
options: TCP provides reliable ordered delivery; UDP is connectionless and does not guarantee delivery
TCP is always faster and never retransmits
correctAnswer: TCP provides reliable ordered delivery; UDP is connectionless and does not guarantee delivery
contentItems.explanation: TCP uses acknowledgements, ordering, and retransmission. UDP is lightweight and connectionless. TCP is not always faster, UDP does not guarantee ordering, and TLS is optional above transport.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which statement best describes TCP compared with UDP?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: TCP Handshake
contentItems.question: What is the correct TCP connection establishment sequence?
correctAnswer: SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK
contentItems.explanation: TCP's three-way handshake starts with SYN, then SYN-ACK, then ACK. FIN closes connections, and DNS/ARP/HTTP are separate protocols.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is the correct TCP connection establishment sequence?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Subnet Mask Role
contentItems.question: What does a subnet mask determine?
options: Which bits identify the network portion of an IP address
correctAnswer: Which bits identify the network portion of an IP address
contentItems.explanation: The subnet mask splits IP address bits into network and host portions. It is unrelated to encryption keys, HTTP status, or DNS TTL alone.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does a subnet mask determine?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: HTTP 401 vs 403
contentItems.question: What is the difference between HTTP 401 and 403?
options: 401 means unauthenticated; 403 means authenticated or identified but not allowed
correctAnswer: 401 means unauthenticated; 403 means authenticated or identified but not allowed
contentItems.explanation: 401 asks for valid authentication; 403 means access is forbidden. Neither implies success, redirect, or necessarily a server crash.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is the difference between HTTP 401 and 403?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: NAT Purpose
contentItems.question: Why is NAT commonly used in home or office networks?
options: To let multiple private hosts share a public IP address
correctAnswer: To let multiple private hosts share a public IP address
contentItems.explanation: NAT translates private addresses to a public address. It does not replace DNS, remove latency, or automatically encrypt payloads.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why is NAT commonly used in home or office networks?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: TCP Congestion Control
contentItems.question: What is the main purpose of TCP congestion control?
options: Avoid overwhelming the network by adjusting sending rate
Convert domain names to IP addresses
correctAnswer: Avoid overwhelming the network by adjusting sending rate
contentItems.explanation: Congestion control adapts transmission to network conditions. DNS resolves names, compression reduces payload size, and DB scheduling is unrelated.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is the main purpose of TCP congestion control?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: TLS Certificate Validation
contentItems.question: Why does a browser validate a server certificate during HTTPS setup?
options: To verify server identity and establish trust before encrypted communication
correctAnswer: To verify server identity and establish trust before encrypted communication
contentItems.explanation: Certificate validation proves the server owns a trusted identity. It does not optimize CSS, choose indexes, or assign IPs.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why does a browser validate a server certificate during HTTPS setup?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Supervised Learning
contentItems.question: What does supervised learning require?
correctAnswer: Labeled examples
contentItems.explanation: Supervised learning maps inputs to known outputs using labeled data. Encryption, DNS, and no-data training are unrelated.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does supervised learning require?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Train/Test Split
contentItems.question: Why do we use a test set?
options: To estimate performance on unseen data
To train longer on the same examples
To convert labels into DNS records
correctAnswer: To estimate performance on unseen data
contentItems.explanation: A test set approximates generalization. It should not be used for training, does not remove all features, and is unrelated to DNS.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why do we use a test set?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Overfitting
contentItems.question: What is overfitting?
options: Model learns training noise and fails to generalize well
correctAnswer: Model learns training noise and fails to generalize well
contentItems.explanation: Overfitting means training performance is high but unseen performance suffers. It is not about having no parameters, dataset size alone, or networking.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is overfitting?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Precision vs Recall
contentItems.question: In a spam classifier, what does high recall mean?
options: Most actual spam emails are detected
Every predicted spam is always correct
correctAnswer: Most actual spam emails are detected
contentItems.explanation: Recall measures how many actual positives are found. Precision measures how many predicted positives are correct. The other options are unrelated.
contentItems.sourceLine: In a spam classifier, what does high recall mean?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: RAG Purpose
contentItems.question: Why is Retrieval-Augmented Generation used with LLMs?
options: To provide relevant external context before generating an answer
To guarantee every answer is always correct
correctAnswer: To provide relevant external context before generating an answer
contentItems.explanation: RAG retrieves useful context and passes it to the model. It does not delete weights, avoid documents, or guarantee correctness.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why is Retrieval-Augmented Generation used with LLMs?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Embeddings
contentItems.question: What does an embedding represent?
options: Data encoded as a vector capturing semantic similarity
correctAnswer: Data encoded as a vector capturing semantic similarity
contentItems.explanation: Embeddings place similar items near each other in vector space. They are not passwords, scheduler values, or certificates.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does an embedding represent?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Gradient Descent
contentItems.question: What does gradient descent do during model training?
options: Updates parameters in a direction that reduces the loss
correctAnswer: Updates parameters in a direction that reduces the loss
contentItems.explanation: Gradient descent uses gradients of the loss to update parameters. It is unrelated to packets, indexes, or encryption.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does gradient descent do during model training?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Data Leakage
contentItems.question: Why is data leakage dangerous?
options: It lets training use information that would not be available in real prediction
It removes the need for validation
correctAnswer: It lets training use information that would not be available in real prediction
contentItems.explanation: Leakage inflates evaluation and fails in production. It does not improve real generalization, encrypt labels, or remove validation needs.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why is data leakage dangerous?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Continuous Integration
contentItems.question: What is the main goal of Continuous Integration?
options: Frequently merge and validate code with automated checks
correctAnswer: Frequently merge and validate code with automated checks
contentItems.explanation: CI encourages frequent integration plus automated builds/tests. It does not avoid testing, rely on manual copying, or replace Git.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is the main goal of Continuous Integration?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Container Image
contentItems.question: What is a container image?
options: An immutable package containing app files, dependencies, and metadata
correctAnswer: An immutable package containing app files, dependencies, and metadata
contentItems.explanation: Images are templates used to create containers. A running instance is a container, not the image; it is not hardware or a transaction.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is a container image?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Kubernetes Reconciliation
contentItems.question: What does Kubernetes reconciliation mean?
options: Controllers continuously move actual state toward desired state
YAML files execute as shell scripts
correctAnswer: Controllers continuously move actual state toward desired state
contentItems.explanation: Kubernetes controllers compare desired and actual state and act to converge them. Pods can restart, root is not required, and YAML is configuration.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does Kubernetes reconciliation mean?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Infrastructure as Code
contentItems.question: Why use Infrastructure as Code?
options: To version, review, and reproduce infrastructure changes
correctAnswer: To version, review, and reproduce infrastructure changes
contentItems.explanation: IaC treats infrastructure definitions like code. It should not bypass security, hide infra, or store secrets in Git.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why use Infrastructure as Code?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Observability Signals
contentItems.question: Which three signals are commonly associated with observability?
correctAnswer: Logs, metrics, and traces
contentItems.explanation: Observability commonly uses logs for events, metrics for numeric trends, and traces for request paths. The other sets belong to web, DB, and TCP concepts.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which three signals are commonly associated with observability?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Blue-Green Deployment
contentItems.question: What is a key benefit of blue-green deployment?
options: Fast rollback by switching traffic back to the previous environment
correctAnswer: Fast rollback by switching traffic back to the previous environment
contentItems.explanation: Blue-green keeps two environments so traffic can switch quickly. Tests are still needed, two environments exist, and bugs can still happen.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is a key benefit of blue-green deployment?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Secret Management
contentItems.question: What is the safest way to handle production secrets in CI/CD?
options: Use a secret manager or protected CI variables with least privilege
Print secrets in logs for debugging
Share one password with all developers
correctAnswer: Use a secret manager or protected CI variables with least privilege
contentItems.explanation: Secret managers and protected variables reduce exposure. Committing, logging, or sharing secrets creates serious security risk.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is the safest way to handle production secrets in CI/CD?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Canary Release
contentItems.question: What is a canary release?
options: Gradually exposing a new version to a small subset of traffic before full rollout
Deploying directly to every user with no monitoring
correctAnswer: Gradually exposing a new version to a small subset of traffic before full rollout
contentItems.explanation: Canary releases reduce blast radius and use monitoring before expanding rollout. The other choices do not describe canary deployment.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is a canary release?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: CIA Triad
contentItems.question: What does the CIA triad stand for?
correctAnswer: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
contentItems.explanation: CIA is the core model for protecting secrecy, correctness, and access. The other options are unrelated acronyms.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does the CIA triad stand for?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: SQL Injection Defense
contentItems.question: What is the primary defense against SQL injection?
correctAnswer: Parameterized queries
contentItems.explanation: Parameterized queries separate SQL code from user data. Concatenation causes injection risk, and UI/CSS changes do not secure queries.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is the primary defense against SQL injection?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: XSS Impact
contentItems.question: What can stored XSS allow an attacker to do?
options: Run malicious JavaScript in victims' browsers when they view stored content
correctAnswer: Run malicious JavaScript in victims' browsers when they view stored content
contentItems.explanation: Stored XSS persists malicious script in content viewed by users. It is not CPU tuning, indexing, or compression.
contentItems.sourceLine: What can stored XSS allow an attacker to do?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Password Hashing
contentItems.question: Why should password hashes use a unique salt?
options: To make identical passwords produce different hashes and resist precomputed attacks
correctAnswer: To make identical passwords produce different hashes and resist precomputed attacks
contentItems.explanation: A salt prevents equal passwords from sharing hashes and weakens rainbow-table attacks. It does not make hashes reversible or disable auth.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why should password hashes use a unique salt?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: CSRF
contentItems.question: What does CSRF exploit?
options: A browser automatically including credentials on unintended cross-site requests
correctAnswer: A browser automatically including credentials on unintended cross-site requests
contentItems.explanation: CSRF abuses authenticated browser state to submit unwanted actions. It is unrelated to compilers, DNS TTL, or indexes.
contentItems.sourceLine: What does CSRF exploit?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: SSRF
contentItems.question: What is Server-Side Request Forgery?
options: Tricking a server into making requests to unintended internal or external resources
correctAnswer: Tricking a server into making requests to unintended internal or external resources
contentItems.explanation: SSRF abuses server-side network access. It is not CSS, transport replacement, or Git branching.
contentItems.sourceLine: What is Server-Side Request Forgery?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Zero Trust
contentItems.question: Which principle best describes zero trust?
options: Never trust by default; continuously verify identity, device, and context
correctAnswer: Never trust by default; continuously verify identity, device, and context
contentItems.explanation: Zero trust assumes no implicit trust and verifies continuously. Trusting internal networks, removing logs, or sharing admin accounts contradicts it.
contentItems.sourceLine: Which principle best describes zero trust?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

contentItems.title: Rate Limiting
contentItems.question: Why is rate limiting important for authentication endpoints?
options: It reduces brute-force and credential-stuffing attempts
correctAnswer: It reduces brute-force and credential-stuffing attempts
contentItems.explanation: Rate limits slow repeated login attempts. They do not affect CSS, guarantee strong passwords, or replace password hashing.
contentItems.sourceLine: Why is rate limiting important for authentication endpoints?
contentItems.referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.
